description: Validate the analytic picture-decisiveness model (e-0014/e-0015) against a REAL matplotlib render, on the corpus's marginal problem geom_06: rasterize its h(x) plot off-screen at 256/512/640 px and count the curve pixels that fall below the y=0 axis (the dip-below-zero falsifier).
motivation: The decisiveness audit converts feature size in data units to pixels analytically, ignoring anti-aliasing, line width and rasterization. A skeptic can object a thin dark dip might smear into the line and vanish. Confirm the analytic px-footprint matches a genuine render. CPU-only, Agg backend.
formal_results: Render geom_06 h(x) with lw=1.5 + gray y=0 axhline at three figure sizes; in the RGBA buffer locate the axis row (gray) and blue curve pixels, count curve pixels strictly below the axis (rows deep x cols wide).
results_description: 256px: dip 2 rows deep x 3 cols wide (sub-threshold). 512px: 5x5 (VISIBLE). 640px: 6x6 (VISIBLE). Matches the analytic model (analytic dip 8.9x5.6 px at 512; robustness sweep flagged 256px as geom_06's sole dropout).
conclusions: The analytic px-footprint decisiveness model is render-faithful: real anti-aliased matplotlib output reproduces the predicted feature visibility, including the predicted 256px borderline for geom_06. The whole picture-decisiveness audit (e-0014, e-0015) is validated against ground-truth rendering, not just a model -- closing the last methodological gap on the construction side of q-0008.
